items.myth: Ridge and Lasso always improve test performance over OLS.
items.reality: Regularization helps when variance or instability dominates; if the true model is simple and data are abundant, OLS or a small λ may be equally good or slightly better on test error.
items.example: With 10,000 samples and 5 weakly correlated features, CV may select λ ≈ 0 and Ridge nearly matches OLS on holdout MSE.

items.myth: Lasso always picks the correct set of important features.
items.reality: Lasso selects a sparse solution that minimizes penalized loss, not necessarily the causal or ground-truth feature set; correlated features and noise affect which coefficients survive.
items.example: Two redundant sensors may trade places across bootstrap resamples while both remain physically relevant.

items.myth: A larger λ always means a better, safer model.
items.reality: Excessive λ over-shrinks coefficients, raising bias and underfitting; validation curves typically show a U-shape in error versus λ.
items.example: At alpha=1e6 every coefficient nears zero and predictions collapse toward the mean, yielding high validation MSE.
